issues.claim: Even if you reject the architectural-invariant frame ... your specific grief over this deprecation is still misplaced.
issues.objection: The Sanskrit 'atha ca' carries the sense of a hypothetical concession Krishna does not himself endorse (per Shankara: 'on the other hand, if — conveys the sense of assumption'). The engineering rendering preserves the conditional structure ('even if you reject ... even on the secular premise that nothing transcends') but the framing 'reject the architectural-invariant frame' could be read as Krishna granting that the previous twenty verses were optional, which is stronger than the actual concession. The verse grants the materialist premise for argument; it does not grant that the imperishable doctrine is a 'frame' one might dispense with. Acceptable as a translation choice given the project's additive-layer mandate; flagging as one minor only.

notes: The three doctrinally load-bearing words in this verse are nitya-jātam, nityam mṛtam, and arhasi (with nainam as the negation). The engineering layer preserves all three: 'born' / 'died' / 'every system you ever shipped became legacy'; 'still misplaced'; 'arhasi' is rendered through the imperative 'is structurally misplaced.' The concessive logic of atha ca is preserved through the conditional 'even if ... still.' Shankara's reading (assumption-of-opponent's-premise) and Ramanuja's reading (identifying-Self-with-body collapses the concessive) are not conflated; the engineering layer leans on Shankara's structural reading. No invented Sanskrit, no misattribution. PASS.
